title: Digital Campus » Episode 01 – Wikipedia: Friend or Foe?

also, looked episode 1 don’t even see mp3 link/download option. i’d love hear podcasts, need go mobile–so would appreciate download option! bill andrews gmu, hist 697 tom scheinfeldt 2nd april, 2007 bill best bet clicking “subscribe” link. take page allows subscribe podcast via itunes, odeo, clients. want download single episodes, simply right click “play links page. let us know problems. tom sage ross 4th april, 2007 […]gmu’s center history new media new podcast launched week half ago: digital campus[…] friday’s pedagogical shuffle 22nd april, 2007 listened first digital campus podcast excellent discussion wikipedia wikis wikipedia used fabian prieto 20th august, 2007 hi. begin listen podcasts. love propose use wikipedia classroom. already done begining work. way, tom´s approach wikipedia, interesting. use spanish version wikipedia, little changes, generally speaking, teach particular writing reading skills. begin teaching discussion history tabs. nirak.net musings lis student information literacy miscellaneous links 7th september, 2007 digital campus episode 01 wikipedia: friend foe? title: Digital Campus » Episode 03 – CI: Cyberinfrastructure
content: episode 03 ci: cyberinfrastructure 4 april, 20072 comments third podcast begins discussion april fools’ pranks, including great one google acquiring oclc, blogs internet foster hoaxes. week’s feature takes look hot topic cyberinfrastructure. also take look turnitin, larger issue plagiarism. links week include librivox, swivel, center’s research tool zotero. featuring: dan cohen, mills kelly, tom scheinfeldt running time: 55:16 download .mp3 categorized google 2 comments “episode 03 ci: cyberinfrastructure” chris grisanti 5th april, 2007 digital campus, thanks spotlighting swivel april 4th podcast. appreciate mills’ data geekiness hope come play around swivel. clarify one points made mills (51:10), swivel currently allows comparing two different data sets, although feature isn’t clearly called access feature, click compare button graph (apple oranges picture) access comparison interface. couple examples interested listeners: iraq civilian vs. coalition deaths: http://swivel.com/graphs/show/8682955 us gdp vs. yearly avg. global temperature http://swivel.com/graphs/show/1016984 wine consumption vs. violent crime: http://swivel.com/graphs/show/1001967 thanks spotlighting us! regards, chris grisanti swiveler chris@swivel.com turnitin.com violating copyrights students? title: Digital Campus » Episode 04 – Welcome to the Social
content: episode 04 welcome social 17 april, 20073 comments social networking sites like facebook play productive role humanities? episode dan plays old fogey, tom mills talk use sites advantageous way. also report recent meetings digital humanities digital museums, discuss google’s maps creative common’s learn initiative. mills dan plot intervention get tom twitter. also discussed igtd, scenemaker, new digital humanities pbwiki. featuring: dan cohen, mills kelly, tom scheinfeldt. running time: 47:57. download mp3. [editor’s note: podcast recorded terrible tragedy virginia tech–thus normal, jovial tone failure mention horrible day. hearts go entire virginia tech community, colleagues center history new media.] categorized copyright, facebook, google, social networking, twitter 3 comments “episode 04 welcome social” sage 19th april, 2007 enjoyed discussion social networking sites, think guys over-estimate ephemeral aspect. facebook, particular, seems like stay; enough market- mind-share among students network effect pretty much keep competitors gaining significant foothold. apparent transitions social networking coolness may demographic issue: myspace becomes uncool students access facebook account peer community. considering (increasing) level sophistication facebook services, i’d surprised fades time soon. mills 20th april, 2007 hi sage: thanks response. course, none us know ephemeral sites won’t remember time friendster coolest cool. anyone even use site also note facebook starting advertise site colleges high schools–that also religious organizations workplaces. facebook community includes lots grownups, still cool place hangout, see seen? mills sage 26th april, 2007 think facebook probably still place several reason. 1) seem committed keeping whatever new functionalities hot moment. 2) way mindshare within segments serve friendster ever good chance alternate services could gain strong position among groups haven’t yet adopted facebook, school high rates use, network effects much overcome. 3) despite growth, facebook able limit horizons social groups geographic institutional affiliations; new church given access facebook part network state u., privacy options limit even facebook community many facebook communities match pre-existing real-world communities), people haven’t already abandoned facebook despite moderately high use faculty, don’t think likely soon. extent, though, think boundary formal persona students interacting teachers informal persona students interacting breaking student’s facebook face much adopted persona student’s classroom face.) certainly unthinkable facebook could go way friendster. sorry, comments closed. subscribe digital campus title: Digital Campus » Episode 13 – Everything in Moderation?
content: episode 13 everything moderation? 21 september, 20073 comments moderated environment email discussion lists still best way scholars communicate others field? time ripe move conversations onto blogs less mediated open formats? that’s week’s debate feature segment. roundup cover news greater competition microsoft office significance new york times dumping pay-for-certain-content model. picks week include great podcast bbc, blog bizarre interesting maps, way overlay historical maps onto current ones. links mentioned: end h-net time strange maps mapmixer running time: 51:59 download .mp3. categorized blogs, maps, microsoft, podcasting, publishing, reading 3 comments “episode 13 everything moderation?” fabian prieto 22nd september, 2007 think main subject communication. h-latam blog, killer app. someday tried create kind community around country studies failed. want point social networking sites allowing create communities information shared. i’m taking group making facebook. people enters group easily, also send specific types information (events example). think h-net left important examples information concerning academic interests true e-mail must remediated. sept. 24th clio wired musings assignment susanld’s weblog 23rd september, 2007 chnm digital campus episode 13 everything moderation? mills kelly, dan cohen tom scheinfedlt talked email discussion lists dave 3rd october, 2007 comments new york times opening content, particularly archives, reminded chris anderson’s long tail idea. would imagine content nyt archive would accessed sporadically. however, there’s much even every story archive gets hits year, hits add ton traffic–not much traffic last two weeks news items receives, good amount nonetheless. open content use traffic generate ad revenue? would likely profitable charging relative handful people directly access content, assuming long tail theory applies context. sorry, comments closed. subscribe digital campus follow us twitter hosts dan cohen amanda french mills kelly stephen robertson tom scheinfeldt one could spend hours listening witty, modern podcasts. american historical association today contact write digital campus chnm@gmu.edu credits fellows /producers 2014-2015 jordan bratt alyssa toby fahringer jannelle legg anne ladyem mcdivitt amanda regan stephanie anne seal 2013-2014 ben hurwitz jannelle legg anne mcdivitt amanda morgan amanda regan spencer roberts ken albers, producer 2007-2008 misha vinokur, producer 2008-2010 mike o'malley, music jeremy boggs, design site powered wordpress categories 3d printing (1) amazon (7) android (6) apple (20) apple watch (2) archives (9) awards (3) blackboard (7) blogs (12) books (28) browsers (6) buddypress (2) cloud computing (4) conferences (10) copyright (22) course management systems (10) data (1) digital dissertations (1) digital humanities (23) dpla (3) ebooks (22) elsevier (6) email (4) facebook (18) flickr (2) freedom speech (3) funding (6) github (1) google (43) gossip (5) hardware (4) intellectual property (14) ipad (9) iphone (11) journals (13) jstor (6) law (10) libraries (25) library congress (7) linked open data (2) linux (4) maps (2) microsoft (18) mobile (15) moocs (11) mozilla (3) museums (9) nara (1) neh (5) net neutrality (3) netbooks (5) omeka (3) open access (30) open source (5) pinterest (2) podcasting (6) privacy (9) programming (6) proquest (1) public domain (5) publishing (32) reading (10) repositories (3) search (8) social networking (21) sustainability (5) syllabi (1) teaching (13) tenure promotion (3) thatcamp (1) tumblr (2) twitter (18) uncategorized (1) unconferences (7) video (4) virtual worlds (5) wearable technology (1) web 2.0 (4) web applications (4) wikipedia (9) wikis (3) wordpress (2) yahoo! digital research tools (dirt) wiki running time: 47:21 download .mp3 categorized books, microsoft, twitter 4 comments “episode 27 atwitter” ken pendergrass 5th june, 2008 fyi- tried call 703-879-4796 number today seattle…kept getting busy signal. think great idea, couldn’t connect. light opening comments microsoft’s pullout digital book scanning business, think would great brewster cale show guest. founder internet archive, mr. cale recently twit podcast spoke length microsoft yahoo’s involvement digital book scanning great insight vision incredible project. tom scheinfeldt 5th june, 2008 thanks, ken. that’s great idea brewster. we’ll see actually working ia zotero commons project. tom mark sample 6th june, 2008 appreciate debate twitter. started uncommitted, like dan, i’m moving closer tom’s position. lately i’ve thinking could use twitter fall transformative teaching tool class devoted new media literature, e.g. students write collaborative “story” dispersed across number twitter posts number users. something tipped balance favor twitter number tools platforms making use twitter’s api. digital writing perspective, there’s twistori, organizes twitterverse six different emotional states (love, hate, think, believe, feel, wish), makes perversely pleasurable voyeuristic reading experience. practically, i’ve experimenting jott, free voice-to-text service. call toll-free number leave message, jott converts (quite accurately) text either sends someone else email, optionally, sends twitter tweet. digital campus listeners interested tools, twitter fan wiki comprehensive list twitter tools worth checking cameron blevins 10th june, 2008 sure saw page twitter librarians: ultimate guide, offers good introduction, interesting ideas, lots good links sources. looking forward recap thatcamp! sorry, comments closed. subscribe digital campus follow us twitter hosts dan cohen amanda french mills kelly stephen robertson tom scheinfeldt one could spend hours listening title: Digital Campus » Episode 28 – Raising the BarCamp
content: episode 28 raising barcamp 17 june, 20082 comments might alternative conventional meetings conferences academics, librarians, museum professionals go every year, papers panels—and often bored distracted attendees—are norm? episode’s feature story tackles question looking back experience thatcamp: humanities technology camp, less structured “unconference” “barcamp” turned everyone active participants. roundtable discussion news includes discussion iphone 3g iphone apps mean educational cultural institutions. picks week include new site soviet gulag, way avoid distractions mac, open source mapping site. links mentioned podcast: thatcamp greennote os x spaces gulag: many days, many lives open street map running time: 45:19 download .mp3 categorized iphone, unconferences 2 comments “episode 28 raising barcamp” bryan alexander 19th june, 2008 good show! question iphone: see limitations campus adoption based at+t service plan (cost, coverage)? (good show far listening stages) tom scheinfeldt 19th june, 2008 thanks, bryan. sense campuses per se won’t adopt iphone phone matter) reasons identify. would tough get george mason budgets people, example, agree let us buy bunch iphones at&t contracts use classrooms even grant projects. however, think new iphone raises bar significantly soon handset makers service providers follow suit. indeed already seeing upcoming release sprint/samsung instinct, we’ll see even fall andriod finally released. pretty soon think students audiences research public projects iphone-like devices pockets already. point we’ll really able take advantage new opportunities afforded iphone’s feature set. certainly don’t expect administrations provide much leadership area. sorry, comments closed. subscribe title: Digital Campus » Episode 44 – Unsettled
content: episode 44 unsettled 30 september, 20098 comments installment digital campus, couldn’t decide happy google mad google. tom, dan, mills confused feelings whole google issue invited two new “irregulars” join us jeff mcclurken amanda french proved unsettled even though didn’t help us much core problem, enjoyed show much we’ve decided ask back show along irregulars named later. five us also discussed future libraries digital age new raft picks check links mentioned podcast: libraries future conference google study tips invincible cities planned obsolescence ted talk: schools kill creativity ted talk: best stats you’ve ever seen jeff mcclurken tim o’donnell’s seminar using ted talks social media governance running time: 51:01 download .mp3 categorized books, google, libraries, microsoft 8 comments “episode 44 unsettled” planned obsolescence digital campus 30th september, 2009 newest episode digital campus podcast, #44 unsettled, i’m thrilled mentions planned obsolescence. digital campus, produced digital campus podcast google books settlement archives public history digital 30th september, 2009 higher education, even (perhaps especially) i’m listen download http://digitalcampus.tv/2009/09/30/episode-44-unsettled/ else launch itunes software, go itunes store, search “digital rick 30th september, 2009 points i’d like raise: 1. reiterating point 20:40 podcast, think crucial way correcting metadata. don’t think necessarily something like wiki, anyone change anything instantly, might costly human reviewer every suggestion, think google could work kind compromise. perhaps make easy add suggestions corrections flip switch 3 people sufficiently different ip addresses make change? 2. old books, scanning clearly way go. newer books would much easier publishers send google final version electronic files printers used produce physical book, would remove need ocr. raises question: publishers willing put electronic files online, would arguably preferable point view rather going google. 3. jeff bezos interview wired years ago suggested could prove owned book, able read entirety online. talk issue? search google books various restrictions see. ownership could tracked, google could remove restrictions books. case, appreciate covering issue. think important one don’t care many times revisit tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 thanks comments, @rick. couple responses. regard second point, believe largely amazon’s strategy kindle: rather scanning books, they’re getting digital files direct publishers. regard third point, argument also made movies music–that preownership analog copy work impute kind digital rights owner–always unsuccessfully. rick 1st october, 2009 tom, link tip? didn’t catch name audio file. tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 good catch. http://socialmediagovernance.com/policies.php maura smale 2nd october, 2009 great episode! couple comments (full disclosure potential biases: i’m academic librarian): 1. re: google books metadata agree google seems prefer programmatic solutions crowdsourcing. seems like missed opportunity ignore 100+ yrs metadata work academic librarians. can’t use i’ve read google’s pulling library records universities metadata problems persist. seems like perfect opportunity use human-generated -corrected) info well automated methods like recaptcha. 2. like many librarians read inside higher ed piece daniel greenstein’s presentation mixture dread amusement. one thing always nags many sweeping proclamations future libraries tend treat library homogenous, monolithic entity. mentioned podcast, public libraries bursting seams days. there’s real, true variation academic libraries, way there’s variation institutions higher ed. libraries major research university (usually single) library community college. public, urban, commuter, technical college work, library busy. students come study hang course, also get fair amount traffic reference desk. mention, library instruction/information literacy important growing) area academic librarians contribute student learning. may need less space physical collections libraries future, think library learning space continue valuable long time yet. jeffrey w. mcclurken welcome! 17th april, 2010 podcast digital technology impact campus life. content: episode 44 unsettled 30 september, 20098 comments installment digital campus, couldn’t decide happy google mad google. tom, dan, mills confused feelings whole google issue invited two new “irregulars” join us jeff mcclurken amanda french proved unsettled even though didn’t help us much core problem, enjoyed show much we’ve decided ask back show along irregulars named later. five us also discussed future libraries digital age new raft picks check links mentioned podcast: libraries future conference google study tips invincible cities planned obsolescence ted talk: schools kill creativity ted talk: best stats you’ve ever seen jeff mcclurken tim o’donnell’s seminar using ted talks social media governance running time: 51:01 download .mp3 categorized books, google, libraries, microsoft 8 comments “episode 44 unsettled” planned obsolescence digital campus 30th september, 2009 newest episode digital campus podcast, #44 unsettled, i’m thrilled mentions planned obsolescence. digital campus, produced digital campus podcast google books settlement archives public history digital 30th september, 2009 higher education, even (perhaps especially) i’m listen download http://digitalcampus.tv/2009/09/30/episode-44-unsettled/ else launch itunes software, go itunes store, search “digital rick 30th september, 2009 points i’d like raise: 1. reiterating point 20:40 podcast, think crucial way correcting metadata. don’t think necessarily something like wiki, anyone change anything instantly, might costly human reviewer every suggestion, think google could work kind compromise. perhaps make easy add suggestions corrections flip switch 3 people sufficiently different ip addresses make change? 2. old books, scanning clearly way go. newer books would much easier publishers send google final version electronic files printers used produce physical book, would remove need ocr. raises question: publishers willing put electronic files online, would arguably preferable point view rather going google. 3. jeff bezos interview wired years ago suggested could prove owned book, able read entirety online. talk issue? search google books various restrictions see. ownership could tracked, google could remove restrictions books. case, appreciate covering issue. think important one don’t care many times revisit tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 thanks comments, @rick. couple responses. regard second point, believe largely amazon’s strategy kindle: rather scanning books, they’re getting digital files direct publishers. regard third point, argument also made movies music–that preownership analog copy work impute kind digital rights owner–always unsuccessfully. rick 1st october, 2009 tom, link tip? didn’t catch name audio file. tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 good catch. http://socialmediagovernance.com/policies.php maura smale 2nd october, 2009 great episode! couple comments (full disclosure potential biases: i’m academic librarian): 1. re: google books metadata agree google seems prefer programmatic solutions crowdsourcing. seems like missed opportunity ignore 100+ yrs metadata work academic librarians. can’t use i’ve read google’s pulling library records universities metadata problems persist. seems like perfect opportunity use human-generated -corrected) info well automated methods like recaptcha. 2. like many librarians read inside higher ed piece daniel greenstein’s presentation mixture dread amusement. one thing always nags many sweeping proclamations future libraries tend treat library homogenous, monolithic entity. mentioned podcast, public libraries bursting seams days. there’s real, true variation academic libraries, way there’s variation institutions higher ed. libraries major research university (usually single) library community college. public, urban, commuter, technical college work, library busy. students come study hang course, also get fair amount traffic reference desk. mention, library instruction/information literacy important growing) area academic librarians contribute student learning. may need less space physical collections libraries future, think library learning space continue valuable long time yet. jeffrey w. mcclurken welcome! 17th april, 2010 podcast digital technology impact campus life. title: Digital Campus » Episode 56 – Past Play
content: episode 56 past play 7 may, 20105 comments sitting offices wishing outside beautiful spring weather, tom, dan, mills took virtual journey north border talk kevin kee bill turkel recent conference playing technology history niagara-on-the-lake, ontario. kevin director simulating history lab brock university bill guru lab humanistic fabrication. addition discussing conference kevin’s bill’s work cutting edge–perhaps even bleeding edge–of digital humanities, also debated pros cons unconference model academic meetings whether thought “play” appropriate objective history teachers. kevin also gave us sneak preview mobile history app team developing coincide bicentennial war 1812. don’t idea “humanistic fabrication” you’ve never pondered whether need makerbot, definitely need listen podcast. links mentioned podcast: history education network/histoire et éducation en réseau craftrobo arduinos rob macdougall barely games running time: 50:01 download .mp3 categorized blogs, mobile, unconferences 5 comments “episode 56 past play” derek bruff 10th may, 2010 thanks sharing #pastplay experiences. particularly liked hearing mobile app developed exploring war 1812. i’m teaching first-year writing seminar history math cryptography fall, i’ve thinking using smart phones part end-of-semester cryptography hunt around campus, perhaps using scvngr blockchalk. hearing kevin’s app, i’m even enthusiastic something along lines! shawn 11th may, 2010 rhetoric game based learning might indeed overblown… many *real* examples game based learning university level examples actual students, actual classes, actual grades whatever incentives actually exist? something meant ask day… shawn where’s beef? digital humanities answer questions? found history 12th may, 2010 back mind later. need time experiment even—as discussed recently bill turkel kevin kee digital campus—time weblog.histnet.ch blog archive playing history 13th may, 2010 perhaps interesting paper draft delivered conference came historian, professor english–steve ramsey university nebraska. ramsey’s paper, million books?” proposes: many books. little time. ethical obligation neither read pretend read understand path vast archive important moment world’s duration—as invitation community, relationship, play.” focusing path rather destination certainly challenge us trained traditional research methods, given swelling flood humanities content online, may well heed ramsey’s argument. drafts papers, one day soon appear book tentatively titled past play, available conference website. discussion title: Digital Campus » Episode 56 – Past Play
content: episode 56 past play 7 may, 20105 comments sitting offices wishing outside beautiful spring weather, tom, dan, mills took virtual journey north border talk kevin kee bill turkel recent conference playing technology history niagara-on-the-lake, ontario. kevin director simulating history lab brock university bill guru lab humanistic fabrication. addition discussing conference kevin’s bill’s work cutting edge–perhaps even bleeding edge–of digital humanities, also debated pros cons unconference model academic meetings whether thought “play” appropriate objective history teachers. kevin also gave us sneak preview mobile history app team developing coincide bicentennial war 1812. don’t idea “humanistic fabrication” you’ve never pondered whether need makerbot, definitely need listen podcast. links mentioned podcast: history education network/histoire et éducation en réseau craftrobo arduinos rob macdougall barely games running time: 50:01 download .mp3 categorized blogs, mobile, unconferences 5 comments “episode 56 past play” derek bruff 10th may, 2010 thanks sharing #pastplay experiences. particularly liked hearing mobile app developed exploring war 1812. i’m teaching first-year writing seminar history math cryptography fall, i’ve thinking using smart phones part end-of-semester cryptography hunt around campus, perhaps using scvngr blockchalk. hearing kevin’s app, i’m even enthusiastic something along lines! shawn 11th may, 2010 rhetoric game based learning might indeed overblown… many *real* examples game based learning university level examples actual students, actual classes, actual grades whatever incentives actually exist? something meant ask day… shawn where’s beef? digital humanities answer questions? found history 12th may, 2010 back mind later. need time experiment even—as discussed recently bill turkel kevin kee digital campus—time weblog.histnet.ch blog archive playing history 13th may, 2010 perhaps interesting paper draft delivered conference came historian, professor english–steve ramsey university nebraska. ramsey’s paper, million books?” proposes: many books. little time. ethical obligation neither read pretend read understand path vast archive important moment world’s duration—as invitation community, relationship, play.” focusing path rather destination certainly challenge us trained traditional research methods, given swelling flood humanities content online, may well heed ramsey’s argument. drafts papers, one day soon appear book tentatively titled past play, available conference website. discussion conference organizers heard digital campus podcast. r&d agenda embodied interaction dh? thatcamp virginia 2010 7th november, 2010 hacking wearables e-textiles thatcamp great lakes, fun stuff #pastplay title: Digital Campus » Episode 57 – Fight Club Soap
content: episode 57 fight club soap 10 june, 20103 comments returning post-thatcamp hiatus, podcast regulars dan, mills, tom joined original irregulars amanda french jeff mcclurken discuss new iphone, nascent course management offering google, launch microsoft office web apps. panel applauds university california/california digital library showdown nature publishing group subscription costs weighs students buying selling course spots craigslist. hat tip good friend bethany nowviskie episode’s inspired title. links mentioned podcast: profhacker letter uc faculty [.pdf] nature publishing group responds, via ars technica running time: 57:41 download .mp3 categorized apple, course management systems, google, iphone, journals, libraries, microsoft, publishing 3 comments “episode 57 fight club soap” peter hirtle 14th june, 2010 thanks early perspectives nature publishing group issue including response web site. cdl’s response http://osc.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/uc_response_to_nature_publishing_group.pdf. two questions, though: 1. appears cdl getting substantial subscription discount years cdl’s response says every school paying much, problem. think “used car pricing” approach scholarly subscriptions i.e., price pay based good negotiator school 2. talked younger scholars want work open access, recent report center studies higher education faculty value needs scholarly communications (found http://escholarship.org/uc/cshe_fsc stressed history faculty, particular younger faculty, willing publish outside traditional venues fear would hurt tenure chances. recent national humanities alliance’s report humanities social science journals http://www.nhalliance.org/news/humanities-social-science-scholarly-journal-publis.shtml) discovered average cost per article journals surveyed ranged $3,000 $21,000+. unlikely historians willing pay costs author-pays model open access. mean willing give editorial services publishers provide? author’s final version article useful final version published journal? perhaps future episode build dan cohen’s thoughtful recent blog postings discuss open access humanities. tom scheinfeldt 14th june, 2010 thanks, peter, excellent comment. like metaphor “used car pricing,” one things makes difficult know truth lies conversations. agree historians would unwilling pay $21,000 publish work, also don’t understand earth cost $21,000 publish history article writing, peer review, much editorial work done volunteer labor distribution online essentially free. trying quantify things admittedly ridiculous, let’s say author’s final version 5% 10% less useful final, published version. last 5% 10% really worth $3k $21k publishers say finally, completely aside questions much adequate editing peer review costs, one also admit journal publishers (especially sciences) aren’t covering costs. they’re making huge profits time rest academic establishment facing huge shortfalls. jeffrey w. mcclurken welcome! 16th june, 2010 digital campus i’m occasional guest “irregular”) podcast digital technology impact campus life. (see episodes 44, 53, 57) sorry, comments closed. subscribe digital campus follow us twitter hosts dan cohen amanda french mills kelly stephen robertson tom scheinfeldt one could spend hours listening witty, modern podcasts. american historical association today contact write digital campus chnm@gmu.edu credits fellows /producers 2014-2015 jordan bratt alyssa toby fahringer jannelle legg anne ladyem mcdivitt amanda regan stephanie anne seal 2013-2014 ben hurwitz jannelle legg anne mcdivitt amanda morgan amanda regan spencer roberts ken albers, producer 2007-2008 misha vinokur, producer 2008-2010 mike o'malley, music jeremy boggs, design site powered wordpress categories 3d printing (1) amazon (7) android (6) apple (20) apple watch (2) archives (9) awards (3) blackboard (7) blogs (12) books (28) browsers (6) buddypress (2) cloud computing (4) conferences (10) copyright (22) course management systems (10) data (1) digital dissertations (1) digital humanities (23) dpla (3) ebooks (22) elsevier (6) email (4) facebook (18) flickr (2) freedom speech (3) funding (6) github (1) google (43) gossip (5) hardware (4) intellectual property (14) ipad (9) iphone (11) journals (13) jstor (6) law (10) libraries (25) library congress (7) linked open data (2) linux (4) maps (2) microsoft (18) mobile (15) moocs (11) mozilla (3) museums (9) nara (1) neh (5) net neutrality (3) netbooks (5) omeka (3) open access (30) open source (5) pinterest (2) podcasting (6) privacy (9) programming (6) proquest (1) public domain (5) publishing (32) reading (10) repositories (3) search (8) social networking (21) sustainability (5) syllabi (1) teaching (13) tenure promotion (3) thatcamp (1) tumblr (2) twitter (18) uncategorized (1) unconferences (7) video (4) virtual worlds (5) wearable technology (1) web 2.0 (4) web applications (4) wikipedia (9) wikis (3) wordpress (2) yahoo! title: Digital Campus » Episode 59 — Digital Replacements
content: episode 59 digital replacements 9 september, 20102 comments fourth annual back-to-school edition digital campus tom, dan, mills invited podcast irregulars amanda french bryan alexander join discussion expect year ahead. mills wondered whether news facebook central ubiquitous social networking platform losing grip college students meant might replaced something new, shot others podcast. speculate potential competitors like diaspora might mean future social networking among students. also wondered whether year e-books begin really replace textbooks campus. sudden demise digital version rice university press also left us wondering whether digital imprints might ever replace bricks mortar/paper glue university press. find concluded possible digital replacements, you’ll sit back listen. links mentioned podcast: run university press clay shirky future print mobile textbooks running time: 54:04 download .mp3 categorized books, facebook, ipad, mobile, publishing 2 comments title: Digital Campus » Episode 66 – The End of Big Search As We Know It?
content: episode 66 end big search know 22 february, 20117 comments edition podcast tom, amanda, dan, mills considered whether recent news stories spammers gaming google search engine algorithm herald end big search know really case google engineers out-coded counterparts “content farms” spam generating locations? mean educators, students, cultural institutions like museums, libraries, archives? also looked q&a site quora weren’t bowled google art project (everyone tom bowled running time: 37:09 download .mp3 categorized digital humanities, google, twitter 7 comments “episode 66 end big search know derek bruff 24th february, 2011 i’ve spent couple minutes quora i’m betting done), i’m still sure valuable resource find little addicting. y’all mentioned one selling points quora one follow quora users. see think real value able hear multiple perspectives questions. see interesting question say, dozen different answers, voted quora users, that’s kind question worth exploring quora. nice complement wikipedia. wikipedia emphasizes facts makes little difficult see contributed quora emphasizes opinions perspectives makes clear who’s saying i’m convinced game changer, certainly interesting model victoria leachman 26th february, 2011 discussion google art project wondering digital campus thought art galleries museums collections online services? want think go future? tom wanted “new kind gallery space web”. i’m really interested hearing say art museums museums art, humanities, natural environment collections… many even quite large significant institutions starting getting images collections basic metadata online. lot institutions trying different things make finding browsing collection easier. cultural institutions getting together bundle collections projects like new zealand’s matapihi also nzmuseums, europe’s europeana, uk’s culture grid etc. access across collecting institutions major thing humanities scholars want something else? tom scheinfeldt 28th february, 2011 @victoria thanks listening! i’m absolutely supportive efforts get collections online make accessible, especially projects aim federate metadata make discovery easier researchers. omeka, omeka.net, omeka commons project aim that—help smaller institutions get collections metadata online provide centralized portal allow researchers discover use collections. don’t think work done get digitized image collection item online. also need better ways interpreting collections telling stories web. basically know put high resolution images artwork online. would like seen google’s unparalleled resources creativity devoted helping us explore make experience high resolution artwork online richer, engaging, critical, educational. sherman dorn 1st march, 2011 okay, time use google art project things museum never could. remember steve martin’s character roller-skating l.a. county museum art “l.a. story”? (okay, probably imagine look favorite art popcorn lap. hereby issue google art project improv challenge tom anyone else wants take public space classroom, challenge people (including wild wacky things right next famous art famous museums. write various activities index cards. shuffle cards. “player” come navigate piece art google art project. player must take card deck respond immediately card piece art improv’s rule accept suggestions). initial suggestions write cards: sing dance orate converse madonna ventriloquism mime charades illuminati fraud marx aristotle eye murder cate h 3rd march, 2011 link “recent articles” google didn’t work found something washington post http://voices.washingtonpost.com/blog-post/2011/01/is_google_getting_less_reliabl.html. one you’re referring matt macarthur 11th march, 2011 listened interested take google art project. nothing google clearly attraction however, i’ve never enamored attempts re-create physical gallery experience online. may partially attempt makes less sense kinds exhibits galleries history museum. you’re right, museums manufactured spaces, museum experience mostly space physical proximity artifacts, obviously impossible duplicate digitally. attempts know create virtual museum “spaces” even allow users create galleries generally fallen flat. case, continue see behaviors expectations online audiences continue quite different physical visitors. relatively online users come “experience” meander might museum, usable, reliable, on-demand information topic interest. one aspect seems unite audiences least desire interaction part experience, whether museum staff, family friends, less often fellow visitors share common interests. regard, think smithsonian commons project trying get ground (http://www.si.edu/commons/prototype/) much interesting model reinvent museum online take advantage unique qualities digital medium. p.s. checked adobe museum digital media? (http://www.adobe.com/adobemuseum/) another interesting experiment regard heard mixed opinions. bryan alexander 31st march, 2011 wonder google scholar searches hit content farms spammers. sorry, title: Digital Campus » Episode 68 – OMG No GBS
content: episode 68 omg gbs 4 april, 20114 comments two big stories dominate edition podcast: rejection google books settlement request professor’s personal email. tom, mills, amanda, dan discuss settlement google authors guild association american publishers didn’t pass muster, ramifications ruling also look in-depth means university-provided email address given wisconsin gop’s request gain access william cronon’s email messages. lighter note, digital campus team tries decide addition omg lol oed spells end civilization. [editor’s note: recorded podcast 3/31/11, resolution cronon affair.] additional links mentioned podcast: ‘academic freedom’ offers little protection new efforts obtain professors’ e-mails tyler cowen’s ethnic dining guide running time: 50:05 download .mp3 categorized books, email 4 comments “episode 68 omg gbs” john muccigrosso 4th april, 2011 dan, corpora, corpora. latinity. believed google “indexing” beard? peter hirtle 10th april, 2011 surprised group academics actually use older material weren’t critical gbs ruling. chin’s decision means full-text access orphan works congress acts. given rights holders won’t even allow books indexed without permission, unlikely would ever support congressional efforts limit rights control use full text content. google’s indexing fair use, destroy copies google hathitrust. goodbye research corpus. google pledged settlement work new orphan works legislation. best solution academia would see gbs approved short-term solution, work equitable solution congress. remember: gbs also solution great problematic issue, namely exactly owns digital rights books. one talked congressional solution mess. one individual among major academic librarians spoke settlement positive step scholarship. one last thing: negotiations secret, reports michael boni authors guild, google, suggested win-win solution google get bookselling business. erik 13th april, 2011 great discussion usual. don’t mean get william f. buckley believe omg lol acronyms, abbreviations initialisms. understanding acronyms pronounceable (e.g. “nato,” “scuba,” “e.g.”). ian thomas 11th may, 2011 could wrong could ferpa risk using students non-.edu email address? though unlikely, wouldn’t difficult make new email address impersonating student attempt gain private information professor. that’s main reason won’t discuss academic information students email address administered university. sorry, comments closed. subscribe digital campus follow us twitter hosts online index indeed transformative therefore fair use. huzzah! way clear kinds things also talk new digital humanities libraries tool called bigdiva launched today, discussing mainly plan become subscription-based paid service. leads brief digression recent patent win wisconsin alumni research foundation apple, could potentially raise almost half billion dollars university wisconsin system enough make proposed budget cuts). refrain comment. finally, stephen robertson reports rrchnm’s plan build new tool called tropy, would help researchers organize pictures take archives. related links title: Digital Campus » Episode #99 — Head and Shoulders Above the Rest
content: episode #99 head shoulders rest 30 september, 2013no comments tom, dan, mills, amanda, stephen returned week’s episode digital campus, joined digital history fellows ben hurwitz jannelle legg. began discussing jstor’s new individual subscription offering, jpass, allows individual users access 1500 journals monthly fee $19.50 $199 annually. panel commended jstor’s efforts, mills expressed concern cost subscription effectively prohibit jstor’s target audience (including adjunct faculty) access. amanda pointed jstor access greatly expanded library institutional subscriptions, many people unaware ways currently receive free access. discussion moved “signals,” performance monitoring software purdue university. signals data-mining program collects information individual students time spent online assignments, completion homework, performance quizzes tests. information used alert students areas strength weakness within academic schedule. program showing early signs success, panel concerned type program encourage students develop independent study skills. next, group examined growing complexity free speech internet two recent news stories. first, facebook ‘likes’ found protected fourth circuit appeals court case involving newly re-elected sheriff six fired deputies. second story involved tenured journalism professor university kansas put leave result controversial tweet. final news story concerned digital footprint shadows us web. story law california requires creation “eraser button” minors. aim give users 18 ability delete content websites, apps online services. contended erasure data, particularly popular sites like facebook, twitter tumblr, could effective hosts expressed skepticism imprints fully erased internet. conclude, patrick murray-john delivered report center release omeka api, allow users connect omeka platforms. note: mistakenly patrick murray-john lead developer omeka. patrick murray-john omeka dev team manager; john flatness lead developer. see http://omeka.org/about/staff/. amanda links stories discussed: jstor individual passes http://www.thedigitalshift.com/2013/09/digital-libraries/jstor-launches-jpass-access-accounts-for-individual-researchers/ coursework nagging software “signals” purdue apparently increases graduation rates http://chronicle.com/blogs/wiredcampus/purdue-u-software-prompt-students-to-study-and-graduate/46853 court rules facebook “likes” free speech http://www.theverge.com/2013/9/18/4744288/appeals-court-rules-that-facebook-likes-are-protected-as-free-speech kansas professor suspended tweet content: episode #113–you can’t trust everything web 13 april, 2015no comments episode digital campus, host mills kelly, along dan cohen, amanda french, stephen robertson discuss role technology classroom history’s teachable moments courtesy us postal service. begin, everyone weighs maya angelou stamp controversy whether quotation inaccuracies getting worse internet. crew discusses recent 2. old books, scanning clearly way go. newer books would much easier publishers send google final version electronic files printers used produce physical book, would remove need ocr. raises question: publishers willing put electronic files online, would arguably preferable point view rather going google. 3. jeff bezos interview wired years ago suggested could prove owned book, able read entirety online. talk issue? search google books various restrictions see. ownership could tracked, google could remove restrictions books. case, appreciate covering issue. think important one don’t care many times revisit tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 thanks comments, @rick. couple responses. regard second point, believe largely amazon’s strategy kindle: rather scanning books, they’re getting digital files direct publishers. regard third point, argument also made movies music–that preownership analog copy work impute kind digital rights owner–always unsuccessfully. rick 1st october, 2009 tom, link tip? didn’t catch name audio file. tom scheinfeldt 1st october, 2009 good catch. http://socialmediagovernance.com/policies.php maura smale 2nd october, 2009 great episode! couple comments (full disclosure potential biases: i’m academic librarian): 1. re: google books metadata agree google seems prefer programmatic solutions crowdsourcing. seems like missed opportunity ignore 100+ yrs metadata work academic librarians. can’t use i’ve read google’s pulling library records universities metadata problems persist. seems like perfect opportunity use human-generated -corrected) info well automated methods like recaptcha. 2. like many librarians read inside higher ed piece daniel greenstein’s presentation mixture dread amusement. one thing always nags many sweeping proclamations future libraries tend treat library homogenous, monolithic entity. mentioned podcast, public libraries bursting seams days. there’s real, true variation academic libraries, way there’s variation institutions higher ed. libraries major research university (usually single) library community college. public, urban, commuter, technical college work, library busy. students come study hang course, also get fair amount traffic reference desk. mention, library instruction/information literacy important growing) area academic librarians contribute student learning. may need less space physical collections libraries future, think library learning space continue valuable long time yet. jeffrey w. mcclurken welcome! 17th april, 2010 podcast digital technology impact campus life. title: Digital Campus » Episode 57 – Fight Club Soap
content: episode 57 fight club soap 10 june, 20103 comments returning post-thatcamp hiatus, podcast regulars dan, mills, tom joined original irregulars amanda french jeff mcclurken discuss new iphone, nascent course management offering google, launch microsoft office web apps. panel applauds university california/california digital library showdown nature publishing group subscription costs weighs students buying selling course spots craigslist. hat tip good friend bethany nowviskie episode’s inspired title. links mentioned podcast: profhacker letter uc faculty [.pdf] nature publishing group responds, via ars technica running time: 57:41 download .mp3 categorized apple, course management systems, google, iphone, journals, libraries, microsoft, publishing 3 comments “episode 57 fight club soap” peter hirtle 14th june, 2010 thanks early perspectives nature publishing group issue including response web site. cdl’s response http://osc.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/uc_response_to_nature_publishing_group.pdf. two questions, though: 1. appears cdl getting substantial subscription discount years cdl’s response says every school paying much, problem. think “used car pricing” approach scholarly subscriptions i.e., price pay based good negotiator school 2. talked younger scholars want work open access, recent report center studies higher education faculty value needs scholarly communications (found http://escholarship.org/uc/cshe_fsc stressed history faculty, particular younger faculty, willing publish outside traditional venues fear would hurt tenure chances. recent national humanities alliance’s report humanities social science journals http://www.nhalliance.org/news/humanities-social-science-scholarly-journal-publis.shtml) discovered average cost per article journals surveyed ranged $3,000 $21,000+. unlikely historians willing pay costs author-pays model open access. mean willing give editorial services publishers provide? author’s final version article useful final version published journal? perhaps future episode build dan cohen’s thoughtful recent blog postings discuss open access humanities. tom scheinfeldt 14th june, 2010 thanks, peter, excellent comment. like metaphor “used car pricing,” one things makes difficult know truth lies conversations. agree historians would unwilling pay $21,000 publish work, also don’t understand earth cost $21,000 publish history article writing, peer review, much editorial work done volunteer labor distribution online essentially free. trying quantify things admittedly ridiculous, let’s say author’s final version 5% 10% less useful final, published version. last 5% 10% really worth $3k $21k publishers say finally, completely aside questions much adequate editing peer review costs, one also admit journal publishers (especially sciences) aren’t covering costs. they’re making huge profits time rest academic establishment facing huge shortfalls. jeffrey w. mcclurken welcome! 16th june, 2010 digital campus i’m occasional guest “irregular”) podcast digital technology impact campus life.
